Binary search: the static aspect
(cid:73) The universe set is totally ordered: (U, ≤)
(cid:73) The used data structure:
(cid:73) the array s[0..n − 1]
(cid:73) s[0] < ... < s[n − 1]
FII, UAIC
Lecture 9
DS 2018/2019
6 / 43

The binary search: the static aspect
Function pos(s[0..n − 1], n, a)
begin
p ← 0; q ← n − 1
m ← (p + q)/2
while (s[m]! = a and p < q) do
if (a < s[m]) then
q ← m − 1
else
p ← m + 1
m ← (p + q)/2
if (s[m] = a) then
return m
else
return −1
end
FII, UAIC
Lecture 9
DS 2018/2019

Binary search: the dynamic aspect
The set S suﬀers update operations (insertion / deletion).
The binary search tree:
(cid:73) In any node v it is stored a value from a totally ordered set.
(cid:73) The values stored in the left subtree of v are lower than the value of
v.
(cid:73) The value of v is less than the values stored in the right subtree of v.

Lecture 9

Binary search trees: sorting
(cid:73) Inorder traversal
Function inorder(v , visit)
begin
if (x == NULL) then
return
else
inorder(v → stg , visit)
visit(v)
inorder(v → drp, visit)
end
(cid:73) Time complexity: O(n)
FII, UAIC
Lecture 9
DS 2018/2019
12 / 43

Binary search trees: searching
Function poz(t, x)
begin
p ← t
while (p! = NULL and p → val! = x) do
if (x < p → val) then
p ← p → stg
else
p ← p → drp
return p
end
(cid:73) Time complexity: O(h), h the height
FII, UAIC
Lecture 9
DS 2018/2019
13 / 43

Predecessor/Successor
(cid:73) Modify the search operation: if the searched value x is not in the
tree, then return:
(cid:73) either the highest value < x (predecessor),
(cid:73) either the smallest value > x (successor).
the predecessor of 18
the successor of 18
FII, UAIC
Lecture 9
DS 2018/2019
14 / 43

Successor
Function successor(t)
begin
if (t → drp! = NULL) then
/*min(t → drp)*/
p ← t → drp
while (p → stg ! = NULL) do
p ← p → stg
return p
else
p ← t → pred
while (p! = NULL and t == p → drp) do
t ← p
p ← p → pred
return p
end
FII, UAIC
Lecture 9
DS 2018/2019
15 / 43

Binary search trees: insertion
(cid:73) Search in the tree the place to insert the new element (similarly with
the search operation).
(cid:73) Add the node with the new information, and the left subtree,
respectively the right one is NULL.
Time complexity: O(h), h the height of the tree.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 9
DS 2018/2019

Binary search trees: insertion
Procedure insBinarySearchTree(t, x)
begin
if (t == NULL) then
new(t); t → val ← x; t → stg ← NULL; t → drp ← NULL
else
p ← t
while (p! = NULL) do
predp ← p
if (x < p → val) then p ← p → stg ;
else
if (x > p → val) then p ← p → drp;
else p ← NULL;
if (predp → val! = x) then
if (x < predp → val) then
/* add x as left child of predp */
else /* add x as right child of predp */ ;
end
FII, UAIC
Lecture 9
DS 2018/2019
17 / 43

Binary search trees: elimination
Search x in the tree t; if it is found, then distinguish the following cases:
(cid:73) Case 1: the node p which stores x has no children;
(cid:73) Case 2: the node p which stores x has a single child;
(cid:73) Case 3: the node p which stores x has both children.
(cid:73) Find the node q which stores the highest value y smaller than x (get
down from p to the left and then to the right as much as possible).
(cid:73) Interchange the values from p and q.
(cid:73) Delete q as in case 1 or 2.
Time complexity: O(h), h the height.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 9
DS 2018/2019

(cid:73) Case 1 or 2
Procedure elimCase1or2(t, predp, p)
begin
if (p == t) then
/* t becomes void or */
/* the only child of t becomes the root */
else
if (p → stg == NULL) then
/* replace in predp, p with p → drp */
else
/* replace in predp, p with p → stg */
end

Binary search trees: elimination
Procedure elimBinarySearchTree(t, x)
begin
if (t! = NULL) then
p ← t; predp ← NULL
while (p! = NULL and p → val! = x) do
predp ← p
if (x < p → val) then p ← p → stg ;
else p ← p → drp;
if (p! = NULL) then
if (p → stg == NULL or p → drp == NULL) then
elimCase1or2(t, predp, p)
else
q ← p → stg ; predq ← p
while (q → drp! = NULL) do
predq ← q; q ← q → drp
p → val ← q → val
elimCase1or2(t, predq, q)
end
FII, UAIC
Lecture 9
DS 2018/2019

Balanced search trees
(cid:73) C is a class of balanced trees if
for any tree t with n vertices from C: h(t) ≤ c log n, c constant.
(cid:73) C is a class of balanced trees O(log n)-stable if
there are algorithms for the operations of search, insertion, deletion in
O(log n), and the resulted trees belong to class C.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 9
DS 2018/2019
27 / 43

AVL trees
(G. Adelson-Velskii, E.M. Landis 1962)
(cid:73) A binary search tree t is a balanced AVL tree if for each vertex v ,
|h(v → stg ) − h(v → drp)| ≤ 1
(cid:73) h(v → stg ) − h(v → drp) is called the balancing factor.
(cid:73) Example:
(cid:73) Lemma
If t is an AVL tree with n internal nodes then h(t) = Θ(log n).
Proof. At class.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 9
DS 2018/2019
28 / 43

AVL trees
(cid:73) Theorem
The class of AVL trees is O(log n) stable.
(cid:73) The search/deletion algorithm
(cid:73) The nodes have also saved the balancing factors (−1, 0, 1).
(cid:73) Store the path from the root to the added/deleted node in a stack
(O(log n)).
(cid:73) Traverse the path stored in the stack in reverse order and rebalance the
unbalanced nodes with one of the operations: left/right rotation
simple/double (O(log n)).
FII, UAIC
Lecture 9
DS 2018/2019

Simple left rotation
Procedure leftRotation(x)
begin
y ← x → drp
x → drp ← y → stg
y → stg ← x
return y
end
(cid:73) Time complexity: O(1)
FII, UAIC
Lecture 9

Insertion: algorithm
Procedure balancing(t, x)
begin
while (x! = NULL) do
/* update the height h(x) */
if (h(x → stg )) ≥ 2 + h(x → drp)) then
if (h(x → stg → stg )) ≥ h(x → stg → drp)) then
rightRotation(t, x)
else
leftRotation(t, x → stg ); rightRotation(t, x)
else
if (h(x → drp)) ≥ 2 + h(x → stg )) then
if (h(x → drp → drp)) ≥ h(x → drp → stg )) then
leftRotation(t, x)
else
rightRotation(t, x → drp); leftRotation(t, x)
x ← pred[x]
end
FII, UAIC
Lecture 9

Advantages/drawbacks of AVL trees
(cid:73) Advantages:
(cid:73) Searching, insertion and deletion takes O(log n) complexity.
(cid:73) Drawbacks:
(cid:73) Additional space for storing the height / the balancing factor.
(cid:73) The re-balancing operations are expensive.
(cid:73) Are favorite when we are making more searches and fewer insertions
and deletions
(cid:73) Applications in Data Analysis, Data Mining
